name: Wiggins, Mark W.
recent_1: Renshaw, P., Wiggins, M. W., & Morrison, B. W. (2026). A cue-based processing approach to differentiating levels of safety performance in remotely piloted aircraft operations. Applied Ergonomics, 130, 104650. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104650
recent_1_synopsis: Drawing on cue-based processing theory, this study examined whether domain-specific cue utilisation, independent of self-reported experience, predicts safety performance among 48 small Visual Line-Of-Sight remotely piloted aircraft (drone) operators. Higher cue utilisation was associated with fewer drone safety events even after controlling for experience. It matters because it extends EXPERTise-style cue assessment to an emerging, rapidly proliferating operational domain where conventional sensory feedback is constrained, offering a selection and training lever for safer uncrewed operations.
recent_2: Auton, J., Deidun, L., Sturman, D., Churruca, K., Morrison, B., Molesworth, B. R. C., & Wiggins, M. (2026). Adapting to renewable energy: A mixed methods exploration of safety culture and training needs in Australia's electricity distribution industry. Journal of Safety Research, 97, 197-206. DOI: 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.022
recent_2_synopsis: Using a mixed-methods design, the team investigated how the transition to distributed renewable generation is reshaping safety culture and training requirements within Australia's electricity distribution sector. The work characterises emerging hazards and competency gaps that arise as the grid becomes more dynamic and decentralised. It matters because it links Wiggins's situation-assessment and expertise framework to the safety implications of energy-system transformation, a high-consequence applied context.
recent_3: Morrison, B. W., Graf, E., Bayl-Smith, P., & Wiggins, M. W. (2025). Like Shooting Phish in a Barrel: Cue Utilization and Cognitive Reflection Aid Performance in Controlled, but Not Naturalistic Phishing Tasks. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 19(1), 32-53. DOI: 10.1177/15553434241296170
recent_3_synopsis: This study tested whether individual differences in phishing cue utilisation and cognitive reflection predict the ability to discriminate genuine from fraudulent emails, comparing a controlled discrimination task against a more naturalistic email-management setting. Both cue utilisation and cognitive reflection aided performance in the controlled task but not under naturalistic conditions, revealing a gap between assessed competence and real-world behaviour. It matters for cybersecurity training because it shows that diagnostic ability does not automatically transfer to ecologically realistic, multitasking contexts.
seminal_1: Wiggins, M., & O'Hare, D. (1995). Expertise in aeronautical weather-related decision making: A cross-sectional analysis of general aviation pilots. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 1(4), 305-320. DOI: 10.1037/1076-898X.1.4.305
seminal_1_synopsis: This cross-sectional study compared the information-acquisition strategies and decision performance of inexperienced, intermediate, and experienced general-aviation pilots using computer-based weather scenarios, information-search tracking, and verbal protocols, showing that expert pilots sample and integrate more diagnostic cues. With approximately 254 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it established the empirical case that weather-related decision expertise is grounded in cue acquisition and use, seeding Wiggins's entire cue-utilisation/EXPERTise research program.
seminal_2: Wiggins, M., & O'Hare, D. (2003). Weatherwise: Evaluation of a Cue-Based Training Approach for the Recognition of Deteriorating Weather Conditions during Flight. Human Factors, 45(2), 337-345. DOI: 10.1518/hfes.45.2.337.27246
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper designed and evaluated Weatherwise, a computer-based, cue-based training system intended to help visual pilots recognise and respond to cues signalling deteriorating in-flight weather, testing it against a control group of 66 pilots at self-report and performance levels. With approximately 136 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it demonstrated that cue-based competence can be deliberately trained rather than only acquired through raw experience, providing the applied training counterpart to Wiggins's diagnostic-expertise theory and a template for later domain-specific cue interventions.
expertise_overview: Wiggins studies the assessment and development of expert cognition in high-consequence operational settings, centred on cue utilisation, the largely non-conscious capacity to associate environmental features with meaningful events that underpins diagnosis, sensemaking, and situation assessment. His EXPERTise theoretical and software framework operationalises this construct, and he relates cue utilisation to outcomes such as cognitive load, cognitive resource consumption, skill acquisition, interruption management, and sustained vigilance. Methodologically he favours behaviour-based and performance measures, computer-based simulation and scenario tasks, eye-tracking and fixation analysis, verbal protocols, and individual-differences/psychometric designs. His domains span aviation and remotely piloted aircraft, medicine (radiology, pathology, anaesthesia/clinical simulation), electrical power and process control, driving, water safety and lifeguarding, marksmanship, and cybersecurity/phishing detection. A recurring theme is differentiating genuine cue-based expertise from mere experience and translating that distinction into selection, training, and interface-design interventions.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Mark W. Wiggins, Professor of Organisational Psychology at Macquarie University (Sydney), developer of the cue-utilisation/EXPERTise framework. Confirmed via the Macquarie University researcher profile (researchers.mq.edu.au/en/persons/mark-wiggins), ResearchGate profile, ORCID 0000-0002-6422-9475, and his Google Scholar profile (user SnqbFVAAAAAJ); affiliation, EXPERTise software, and co-author network (O'Hare, Morrison, Auton, Sturman, Molesworth) all align with the ground-truth Human Factors papers. Citation counts are from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17 (profile totals: ~4,506 citations, h-index 38). Seminal_1 (1995) ~254 citations and seminal_2 (2003, Weatherwise) ~136 citations per Google Scholar on 2026-06-17; these are approximate Scholar figures and may differ from other indexes. All 5 cited DOIs (3 recent + 2 seminal) were verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 and returned HTTP 200 with matching titles, authors, and venues. Note on verification: an initial guessed DOI for the Journal of Safety Research paper (10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.001) resolved to an unrelated article, so the correct DOI 10.1016/j.jsr.2026.01.022 was located and confirmed before inclusion. No unverifiable citations were included.
